question:
14. Prove that
\[
\left(\begin{array}{lllll}
a_{1}^{2}+k & a_{1} a_{2} & a_{1} a_{3} & \ldots & a_{1} a_{n} \\
a_{2} a_{1} & a_{2}^{2}+k & a_{2} a_{3} & \ldots & a_{2} a_{n} \\
\ldots & \ldots & \ldots & \ldots & \ldots \\
a_{n} a_{1} & a_{n} a_{2} & a_{n} a_{3} & \ldots & a_{n}^{2}+k
\end{array}\right)
\]
is divisible by \( \boldsymbol{k}^{n-1} \) and find its other factor.
solution:
First Solution. Let \( B \) be the matrix
\[
\left(\begin{array}{lllll}
a_{1}^{2} & a_{1} a_{2} & a_{1} a_{3} & \cdots & a_{1} a_{n} \\
a_{2} a_{1} & a_{2}^{2} & a_{2} a_{3} & \cdots & a_{2} a_{n} \\
\cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots \\
a_{n} a_{1} & a_{n} a_{2} & a_{n} a_{3} & \cdots & a_{n}^{2}
\end{array}\right)
\]
\( B \) has rank at most one, since any two rows (or columns) are clearly dependent. So there are \( (n-1) \) zeros among the eigenvalues of \( B \). Therefore the characteristic polynomial of \( B \) is divisible by \( x^{n-1} \). Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{det}(x \cdot I-B) & =x^{n}-(\operatorname{trace} B) x^{n-1} \\
& =x^{n-1}\left(x-a_{1}{ }^{2}-a_{2}{ }^{2}-\cdots-a_{n}{ }^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]
so
\[
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{det}(B+k I) & =(-1)^{n} \operatorname{det}(-k I-B) \\
& =k^{n-1}\left(k+a_{1}{ }^{2}+a_{2}{ }^{2}+\cdots+a_{n}{ }^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]
and the other factor is \( \left(k+a_{1}{ }^{2}+a_{2}{ }^{2}+\cdots+a_{n}{ }^{2}\right) \).
Second Solution. Assume for a moment that none of the \( a \) 's are zero, and let
\[
B_{n}=\left(\begin{array}{lllll}
a_{1}{ }^{2}+k & a_{1} a_{2} & a_{1} a_{3} & \cdots & a_{1} a_{n} \\
a_{2} a_{1} & a_{2}{ }^{2}+k & a_{2} a_{3} & \cdots & a_{2} a_{n} \\
\cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots \\
a_{n} a_{1} & a_{n} a_{2} & a_{n} a_{3} & \cdots & a_{n}{ }^{2}+k
\end{array}\right)
\]

Since the determinant is linear in the last row, we find

Now in the first of these new determinants, subtract multiples of the last rows from the others to get
\[
\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{lll:l}
k & & 0 & 0 \\
0 & k & k & 0 \\
\hdashline a_{n} a_{1} & a_{n} a_{n-1} & a_{n}^{2}
\end{array}\right) .
\]

Then we have
\[
\operatorname{det} B_{n}=k^{n-1} a_{n}^{2}+k \operatorname{det} B_{n-1}
\]

Since \( \operatorname{det} B_{1}=k+a_{1}{ }^{2} \), the relation
\[
\operatorname{det} B_{n}=k^{n-1}\left(k+a_{1}^{2}+\cdots+a_{n}^{2}\right)
\]
follows easily by induction.
Although this derivation depends on the assumption that the \( a \) 's are not zero, the result remains valid for the case where some of the \( a \) 's are zero, since \( D_{n} \) is evidently some polynomial in \( k \) and the \( a \) 's which agrees with \( k^{n-1}\left(k+a_{1}{ }^{2}+\cdots+a_{n}{ }^{2}\right) \) as long as none of the \( a \) 's are zero. Therefore (1) must be a polynomial identity.

Alternatively, we can regard the computation as taking place in the field \( Q\left(k, a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots, a_{n}\right) \) where \( k \) and the \( a_{i} \) are independent indeterminates. In this field the condition \( a_{i} \neq 0 \) is satisfied.

For a discussion of such fields, see I. N. Herstein, Topics in Algebra, Blaisdell, Waltham, Mass., 1964.

descriptive_long.question:
14. Prove that
\[
\left(\begin{array}{lllll}
coeffone^{2}+constk & coeffone coefftwo & coeffone coeffthr & \ldots & coeffone coefflast \\
coefftwo coeffone & coefftwo^{2}+constk & coefftwo coeffthr & \ldots & coefftwo coefflast \\
\ldots & \ldots & \ldots & \ldots & \ldots \\
coefflast coeffone & coefflast coefftwo & coefflast coeffthr & \ldots & coefflast^{2}+constk
\end{array}\right)
\]
is divisible by \( \boldsymbol{constk}^{sizenum-1} \) and find its other factor.
descriptive_long.solution:
First Solution. Let \( matrixb \) be the matrix
\[
\left(\begin{array}{lllll}
coeffone^{2} & coeffone coefftwo & coeffone coeffthr & \cdots & coeffone coefflast \\
coefftwo coeffone & coefftwo^{2} & coefftwo coeffthr & \cdots & coefftwo coefflast \\
\cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots \\
coefflast coeffone & coefflast coefftwo & coefflast coeffthr & \cdots & coefflast^{2}
\end{array}\right)
\]
\( matrixb \) has rank at most one, since any two rows (or columns) are clearly dependent. So there are \( (sizenum-1) \) zeros among the eigenvalues of \( matrixb \). Therefore the characteristic polynomial of \( matrixb \) is divisible by \( charvar^{sizenum-1} \). Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{det}(charvar \cdot identmx-matrixb) & =charvar^{sizenum}-(\operatorname{trace} matrixb) charvar^{sizenum-1} \\
& =charvar^{sizenum-1}\left(charvar-coeffone^{2}-coefftwo^{2}-\cdots-coefflast^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]
so
\[
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{det}(matrixb+constk\,identmx) & =(-1)^{sizenum} \, \operatorname{det}(-constk\,identmx-matrixb) \\
& =constk^{sizenum-1}\left(constk+coeffone^{2}+coefftwo^{2}+\cdots+coefflast^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]
and the other factor is \( \left(constk+coeffone^{2}+coefftwo^{2}+\cdots+coefflast^{2}\right) \).

Second Solution. Assume for a moment that none of the \( genericco \) 's are zero, and let
\[
matrixbn=\left(\begin{array}{lllll}
coeffone^{2}+constk & coeffone coefftwo & coeffone coeffthr & \cdots & coeffone coefflast \\
coefftwo coeffone & coefftwo^{2}+constk & coefftwo coeffthr & \cdots & coefftwo coefflast \\
\cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots \\
coefflast coeffone & coefflast coefftwo & coefflast coeffthr & \cdots & coefflast^{2}+constk
\end{array}\right)
\]
Since the determinant is linear in the last row, we find

Now in the first of these new determinants, subtract multiples of the last rows from the others to get
\[
\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{lll:l}
constk & & 0 & 0 \\
0 & constk & constk & 0 \\
\hdashline coefflast coeffone & coefflast a_{n-1} & coefflast^{2}
\end{array}\right) .
\]
Then we have
\[
\operatorname{det} matrixbn = constk^{sizenum-1} coefflast^{2} + constk \, \operatorname{det} matrixbnm
\]
Since \( \operatorname{det} matrixb_{1} = constk + coeffone^{2} \), the relation
\[
\operatorname{det} matrixbn = constk^{sizenum-1}\left(constk+coeffone^{2}+\cdots+coefflast^{2}\right)
\]
follows easily by induction.

Although this derivation depends on the assumption that the \( genericco \) 's are not zero, the result remains valid for the case where some of the \( genericco \) 's are zero, since \( determin \) is evidently some polynomial in \( constk \) and the \( genericco \) 's which agrees with \( constk^{sizenum-1}\left(constk+coeffone^{2}+\cdots+coefflast^{2}\right) \) as long as none of the \( genericco \) 's are zero. Therefore (1) must be a polynomial identity.

Alternatively, we can regard the computation as taking place in the field \( fieldrat\left(constk, coeffone, coefftwo, \ldots, coefflast\right) \) where \( constk \) and the \( coeffindi \) are independent indeterminates. In this field the condition \( coeffindi \neq 0 \) is satisfied.

For a discussion of such fields, see I. N. Herstein, Topics in Algebra, Blaisdell, Waltham, Mass., 1964.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
14. Prove that
\[
\left(\begin{array}{lllll}
driftwood^{2}+peppermint & driftwood moonshine & driftwood earthworm & \ldots & driftwood breadcrumb \\
moonshine driftwood & moonshine^{2}+peppermint & moonshine earthworm & \ldots & moonshine breadcrumb \\
\ldots & \ldots & \ldots & \ldots & \ldots \\
breadcrumb driftwood & breadcrumb moonshine & breadcrumb earthworm & \ldots & breadcrumb^{2}+peppermint
\end{array}\right)
\]
is divisible by \( \boldsymbol{peppermint}^{floodgate-1} \) and find its other factor.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
First Solution. Let \( shoelace \) be the matrix
\[
\left(\begin{array}{lllll}
driftwood^{2} & driftwood moonshine & driftwood earthworm & \cdots & driftwood breadcrumb \\
moonshine driftwood & moonshine^{2} & moonshine earthworm & \cdots & moonshine breadcrumb \\
\cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots \\
breadcrumb driftwood & breadcrumb moonshine & breadcrumb earthworm & \cdots & breadcrumb^{2}
\end{array}\right)
\]
\( shoelace \) has rank at most one, since any two rows (or columns) are clearly dependent. So there are \( (floodgate-1) \) zeros among the eigenvalues of \( shoelace \). Therefore the characteristic polynomial of \( shoelace \) is divisible by \( latitude^{floodgate-1} \). Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{det}(latitude \cdot raincloud-shoelace) & =latitude^{floodgate}-(\operatorname{trace} shoelace) latitude^{floodgate-1} \\
& =latitude^{floodgate-1}\left(latitude-driftwood{ }^{2}-moonshine{ }^{2}-\cdots-breadcrumb{ }^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]
so
\[
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{det}(shoelace+peppermint raincloud) & =(-1)^{floodgate} \operatorname{det}(-peppermint raincloud-shoelace) \\
& =peppermint^{floodgate-1}\left(peppermint+driftwood{ }^{2}+moonshine{ }^{2}+\cdots+breadcrumb{ }^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]
and the other factor is \( \left(peppermint+driftwood{ }^{2}+moonshine{ }^{2}+\cdots+breadcrumb{ }^{2}\right) \).

Second Solution. Assume for a moment that none of the \( caravanser \)'s are zero, and let
\[
crosswalk=\left(\begin{array}{lllll}
driftwood{ }^{2}+peppermint & driftwood moonshine & driftwood earthworm & \cdots & driftwood breadcrumb \\
moonshine driftwood & moonshine{ }^{2}+peppermint & moonshine earthworm & \cdots & moonshine breadcrumb \\
\cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots \\
breadcrumb driftwood & breadcrumb moonshine & breadcrumb earthworm & \cdots & breadcrumb{ }^{2}+peppermint
\end{array}\right)
\]

Since the determinant is linear in the last row, we find

Now in the first of these new determinants, subtract multiples of the last rows from the others to get
\[
\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{lll:l}
peppermint & & 0 & 0 \\
0 & peppermint & peppermint & 0 \\
\hdashline breadcrumb driftwood & breadcrumb sailcloth & breadcrumb^{2}
\end{array}\right) .
\]

Then we have
\[
\operatorname{det} crosswalk=peppermint^{floodgate-1} breadcrumb^{2}+peppermint \operatorname{det} overglaze
\]

Since \( \operatorname{det} B_{1}=peppermint+driftwood{ }^{2} \), the relation
\[
\operatorname{det} crosswalk=peppermint^{floodgate-1}\left(peppermint+driftwood^{2}+\cdots+breadcrumb^{2}\right)
\]
follows easily by induction.
Although this derivation depends on the assumption that the \( caravanser \)'s are not zero, the result remains valid for the case where some of the \( caravanser \)'s are zero, since \( thumbtack \) is evidently some polynomial in \( peppermint \) and the \( caravanser \)'s which agrees with \( peppermint^{floodgate-1}\left(peppermint+driftwood{ }^{2}+\cdots+breadcrumb{ }^{2}\right) \) as long as none of the \( caravanser \)'s are zero. Therefore (1) must be a polynomial identity.

Alternatively, we can regard the computation as taking place in the field \( marshland\left(peppermint, driftwood, moonshine, \ldots, breadcrumb\right) \) where \( peppermint \) and the \( soapstone \) are independent indeterminates. In this field the condition \( soapstone \neq 0 \) is satisfied.

For a discussion of such fields, see I. N. Herstein, Topics in Algebra, Blaisdell, Waltham, Mass., 1964.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
14. Prove that
\[
\left(\begin{array}{lllll}
lastcoeff^{2}+variablestar & lastcoeff penultimate & lastcoeff antepenult & \ldots & lastcoeff initialcoef \\
penultimate lastcoeff & penultimate^{2}+variablestar & penultimate antepenult & \ldots & penultimate initialcoef \\
\ldots & \ldots & \ldots & \ldots & \ldots \\
initialcoef lastcoeff & initialcoef penultimate & initialcoef antepenult & \ldots & initialcoef^{2}+variablestar
\end{array}\right)
\]
is divisible by \( \boldsymbol{variablestar}^{tinysize-1} \) and find its other factor.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
First Solution. Let \( scalaronly \) be the matrix
\[
\left(\begin{array}{lllll}
lastcoeff^{2} & lastcoeff penultimate & lastcoeff antepenult & \cdots & lastcoeff initialcoef \\
penultimate lastcoeff & penultimate^{2} & penultimate antepenult & \cdots & penultimate initialcoef \\
\cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots \\
initialcoef lastcoeff & initialcoef penultimate & initialcoef antepenult & \cdots & initialcoef^{2}
\end{array}\right)
\]
\( scalaronly \) has rank at most one, since any two rows (or columns) are clearly dependent. So there are \( (tinysize-1) \) zeros among the eigenvalues of \( scalaronly \). Therefore the characteristic polynomial of \( scalaronly \) is divisible by \( knownvalue^{tinysize-1} \). Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{det}(knownvalue \cdot zeromatrix-scalaronly) & =knownvalue^{tinysize}-(\operatorname{trace} scalaronly) knownvalue^{tinysize-1} \\
& =knownvalue^{tinysize-1}\left(knownvalue-lastcoeff{ }^{2}-penultimate{ }^{2}-\cdots-initialcoef{ }^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]
so
\[
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{det}(scalaronly+variablestar\, zeromatrix) & =(-1)^{tinysize} \operatorname{det}(-variablestar\, zeromatrix-scalaronly) \\
& =variablestar^{tinysize-1}\left(variablestar+lastcoeff{ }^{2}+penultimate{ }^{2}+\cdots+initialcoef{ }^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]
and the other factor is \( \left(variablestar+lastcoeff{ }^{2}+penultimate{ }^{2}+\cdots+initialcoef{ }^{2}\right) \).

Second Solution. Assume for a moment that none of the fixedscalar's are zero, and let
\[
vectorsolo=\left(\begin{array}{lllll}
lastcoeff{ }^{2}+variablestar & lastcoeff penultimate & lastcoeff antepenult & \cdots & lastcoeff initialcoef \\
penultimate lastcoeff & penultimate{ }^{2}+variablestar & penultimate antepenult & \cdots & penultimate initialcoef \\
\cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots \\
initialcoef lastcoeff & initialcoef penultimate & initialcoef antepenult & \cdots & initialcoef{ }^{2}+variablestar
\end{array}\right)
\]

Since the determinant is linear in the last row, we find

Now in the first of these new determinants, subtract multiples of the last rows from the others to get
\[
\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{lll:l}
variablestar & & 0 & 0 \\
0 & variablestar & variablestar & 0 \\
\hdashline initialcoef lastcoeff & initialcoef primaryone & initialcoef^{2}
\end{array}\right) .
\]

Then we have
\[
\operatorname{det} vectorsolo=variablestar^{tinysize-1} initialcoef^{2}+variablestar \operatorname{det} vectorprior
\]

Since \( \operatorname{det} B_{1}=variablestar+lastcoeff{ }^{2} \), the relation
\[
\operatorname{det} vectorsolo=variablestar^{tinysize-1}\left(variablestar+lastcoeff^{2}+\cdots+initialcoef^{2}\right)
\]
follows easily by induction.
Although this derivation depends on the assumption that the fixedscalar's are not zero, the result remains valid for the case where some of the fixedscalar's are zero, since \( integralset \) is evidently some polynomial in variablestar and the fixedscalar's which agrees with \( variablestar^{tinysize-1}\left(variablestar+lastcoeff{ }^{2}+\cdots+initialcoef{ }^{2}\right) \) as long as none of the fixedscalar's are zero. Therefore (1) must be a polynomial identity.

Alternatively, we can regard the computation as taking place in the field \( irrational\left(variablestar, lastcoeff, penultimate, \ldots, initialcoef\right) \) where \( variablestar \) and the \( variableidx \) are independent indeterminates. In this field the condition \( variableidx \neq 0 \) is satisfied.

For a discussion of such fields, see I. N. Herstein, Topics in Algebra, Blaisdell, Waltham, Mass., 1964.

garbled_string.question:
14. Prove that
\[
\left(\begin{array}{lllll}
mczbtequ^{2}+lzvmuyhc & mczbtequ rdlwopsa & mczbtequ vynkfgzh & \ldots & mczbtequ fqhxaljo \\
rdlwopsa mczbtequ & rdlwopsa^{2}+lzvmuyhc & rdlwopsa vynkfgzh & \ldots & rdlwopsa fqhxaljo \\
\ldots & \ldots & \ldots & \ldots & \ldots \\
fqhxaljo mczbtequ & fqhxaljo rdlwopsa & fqhxaljo vynkfgzh & \ldots & fqhxaljo^{2}+lzvmuyhc
\end{array}\right)
\]
is divisible by \( \boldsymbol{lzvmuyhc}^{epqsdktj-1} \) and find its other factor.
garbled_string.solution:
First Solution. Let \( njcwroae \) be the matrix
\[
\left(\begin{array}{lllll}
mczbtequ^{2} & mczbtequ rdlwopsa & mczbtequ vynkfgzh & \cdots & mczbtequ fqhxaljo \\
rdlwopsa mczbtequ & rdlwopsa^{2} & rdlwopsa vynkfgzh & \cdots & rdlwopsa fqhxaljo \\
\cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots \\
fqhxaljo mczbtequ & fqhxaljo rdlwopsa & fqhxaljo vynkfgzh & \cdots & fqhxaljo^{2}
\end{array}\right)
\]
\( njcwroae \) has rank at most one, since any two rows (or columns) are clearly dependent. So there are \( (epqsdktj-1) \) zeros among the eigenvalues of \( njcwroae \). Therefore the characteristic polynomial of \( njcwroae \) is divisible by \( qzxwvtnp^{epqsdktj-1} \). Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{det}(qzxwvtnp \cdot pavhxlge-njcwroae) & =qzxwvtnp^{epqsdktj}-(\operatorname{trace} njcwroae) qzxwvtnp^{epqsdktj-1} \\
& =qzxwvtnp^{epqsdktj-1}\left(qzxwvtnp-mczbtequ^{2}-rdlwopsa^{2}-\cdots-fqhxaljo^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]
so
\[
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{det}(njcwroae+lzvmuyhc pavhxlge) & =(-1)^{epqsdktj} \operatorname{det}(-lzvmuyhc pavhxlge-njcwroae) \\
& =lzvmuyhc^{epqsdktj-1}\left(lzvmuyhc+mczbtequ^{2}+rdlwopsa^{2}+vynkfgzh^{2}+\cdots+fqhxaljo^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]
and the other factor is \( \left(lzvmuyhc+mczbtequ^{2}+rdlwopsa^{2}+\cdots+fqhxaljo^{2}\right) \).

Second Solution. Assume for a moment that none of the \( hjgrksla \) 's are zero, and let
\[
xutdrclm=\left(\begin{array}{lllll}
mczbtequ^{2}+lzvmuyhc & mczbtequ rdlwopsa & mczbtequ vynkfgzh & \cdots & mczbtequ fqhxaljo \\
rdlwopsa mczbtequ & rdlwopsa^{2}+lzvmuyhc & rdlwopsa vynkfgzh & \cdots & rdlwopsa fqhxaljo \\
\cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots \\
fqhxaljo mczbtequ & fqhxaljo rdlwopsa & fqhxaljo vynkfgzh & \cdots & fqhxaljo^{2}+lzvmuyhc
\end{array}\right)
\]

Since the determinant is linear in the last row, we find

Now in the first of these new determinants, subtract multiples of the last rows from the others to get
\[
\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{lll:l}
lzvmuyhc & & 0 & 0 \\
0 & lzvmuyhc & lzvmuyhc & 0 \\
\hdashline fqhxaljo mczbtequ & fqhxaljo ksivpurm & fqhxaljo^{2}
\end{array}\right) .
\]

Then we have
\[
\operatorname{det} xutdrclm=lzvmuyhc^{epqsdktj-1} fqhxaljo^{2}+lzvmuyhc \operatorname{det} sybqomtn
\]

Since \( \operatorname{det} xutdrclm_{1}=lzvmuyhc+mczbtequ^{2} \), the relation
\[
\operatorname{det} xutdrclm=lzvmuyhc^{epqsdktj-1}\left(lzvmuyhc+mczbtequ^{2}+\cdots+fqhxaljo^{2}\right)
\]
follows easily by induction.
Although this derivation depends on the assumption that the \( hjgrksla \) 's are not zero, the result remains valid for the case where some of the \( hjgrksla \) 's are zero, since \( bzvawxye \) is evidently some polynomial in \( lzvmuyhc \) and the \( hjgrksla \) 's which agrees with \( lzvmuyhc^{epqsdktj-1}\left(lzvmuyhc+mczbtequ^{2}+\cdots+fqhxaljo^{2}\right) \) as long as none of the \( hjgrksla \) 's are zero. Therefore (1) must be a polynomial identity.

Alternatively, we can regard the computation as taking place in the field \( wlsrcfuv\left(lzvmuyhc, mczbtequ, rdlwopsa, \ldots, fqhxaljo\right) \) where \( lzvmuyhc \) and the \( ztemwqub \) are independent indeterminates.

For a discussion of such fields, see I. N. Herstein, Topics in Algebra, Blaisdell, Waltham, Mass., 1964.

kernel_variant.question:
Let m and r be integers with 1 \leq  r < m.  
For k = 1,\ldots ,r fix (not necessarily distinct) real column-vectors  

  u^{(k)} = (u_1^{(k)},\ldots ,u_m^{(k)})^t,  v^{(k)} = (v_1^{(k)},\ldots ,v_m^{(k)})^t

and assemble the two m \times  r matrices  

  U := [u^{(1)} u^{(2)} \ldots  u^(r^)],  V := [v^{(1)} v^{(2)} \ldots  v^(r^)].

For indeterminates t_0,t_1,\ldots ,t_r put  

  T := diag(t_1,\ldots ,t_r),  A(t_0,\ldots ,t_r) := t_0I_m + U T V^t.     (1)

All algebraic statements below are meant in the polynomial ring  
\mathbb{R}[t_0,\ldots ,t_r, u_i^{(k)}, v_j^{(k)}].

1. (Factorisation of the determinant)  
 Prove that det A is divisible by t_0^{m-r} and that  

  det A(t_0,\ldots ,t_r) = t_0^{\,m-r} det(t_0 I_r + T V^tU).        (2)

2. (Characteristic polynomial)  
 Let \chi _A(\lambda ) := det(\lambda  I_m - A). Show that  

  \chi _A(\lambda ) = (\lambda -t_0)^{\,m-r} \cdot  det[(\lambda -t_0) I_r - T V^tU].      (3)

3. (Spectrum in the general case)  
 Using (3) describe all eigenvalues of A(t_0,\ldots ,t_r) for arbitrary real
choices of the parameters, and give their algebraic multiplicities.

4. From now on impose the additional non-degeneracy conditions  

  det T \neq  0  (i.e. t_k \neq  0 for k = 1,\ldots ,r)  and  det V^tU \neq  0,

and fix a real number t_0 \neq  0 that is not the negative of any eigenvalue of T V^tU  
(equivalently det(t_0 I_r + T V^tU) \neq  0).

 (a)  Prove that A(t_0,\ldots ,t_r) is invertible and establish the
Woodbury-type formula  

  A^{-1} = t_0^{-1}\Bigl[I_m - U\bigl(t_0 T^{-1}+V^tU\bigr)^{-1}V^t\Bigr]. (4)

 (b)  Compute the adjugate of A:

  adj A  
  = t_0^{\,m-r-1} det T \cdot  det\bigl(t_0 T^{-1}+V^tU\bigr)  
    \times  \Bigl[I_m - U\bigl(t_0 T^{-1}+V^tU\bigr)^{-1}V^t\Bigr].     (5)

5. (Symmetric orthonormal specialisation)  
 Assume in addition that U = V and that the r columns of U are
orthonormal, i.e. U^tU = I_r.

 (a)  Prove that A is symmetric and construct an orthonormal basis
of \mathbb{R}^m consisting of eigenvectors of A.

 (b)  Determine the complete spectrum of A and, for every real
specialisation of the parameters, give the algebraic and geometric
multiplicities of the eigenvalues. In particular show that

  spec A = {t_0 (mult m-r)} \cup  {t_0+t_k  (k = 1,\ldots ,r)},     (6)

and discuss in detail how both multiplicities change when some of the
parameters t_k coincide.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we repeatedly use Sylvester's determinant identity  

 det(I_m + U C V^t) = det(I_r + C V^tU)                (S)

which holds for all conformable matrices over any commutative ring.

1.  Put C := t_0^{-1}T.  Then (1) rewrites as  

 A = t_0(I_m + U C V^t).                    (7)

Taking determinants and applying (S) gives  

 det A = t_0^{m} det(I_m + U C V^t)  
     = t_0^{m} det(I_r + C V^tU)  
     = t_0^{m} det(I_r + t_0^{-1}T V^tU)  
     = t_0^{m-r} det(t_0I_r + T V^tU),  

establishing (2) and the divisibility by t_0^{m-r}.

2.  Put \mu  := \lambda -t_0.  Then  

 \lambda I_m - A = \mu I_m - U T V^t  
      = \mu [I_m - U(T/\mu )V^t].  

Thus  

 \chi _A(\lambda ) = \mu ^{\,m} det(I_m - U(T/\mu )V^t).  

Apply (S) with C := -T/\mu  to obtain  

 \chi _A(\lambda ) = \mu ^{\,m} det(I_r - (T/\mu ) V^tU)  
     = \mu ^{\,m-r} det(\mu  I_r - T V^tU)  
     = (\lambda -t_0)^{\,m-r} det[(\lambda -t_0)I_r - T V^tU],  

which is (3).

3.  Denote by \mu _1,\ldots ,\mu _r the (possibly repeated) eigenvalues of the
r \times  r matrix T V^tU.  Formula (3) implies

 *  \lambda  = t_0 is an eigenvalue of A with algebraic multiplicity m-r.  
 *  For each j = 1,\ldots ,r the number \lambda  = t_0+\mu _j is an eigenvalue of A,
  having the same multiplicity as \mu _j for T V^tU.

When several \mu _j coincide their multiplicities add, and if some \mu _j = 0
the corresponding eigenvalue merges with \lambda  = t_0; the combined
multiplicity is the sum of the individual ones.

4(a).  Because det(t_0I_r+T V^tU) \neq  0 by hypothesis, the factor in (2) is
non-zero, hence det A \neq  0 and A is invertible.

Write again A = t_0(I_m + U C V^t) with C = t_0^{-1}T.  Our additional
assumption det T \neq  0 guarantees that C is invertible, so the Woodbury
identity applies:

 (I_m + U C V^t)^{-1} = I_m - U(C^{-1}+V^tU)^{-1}V^t.

Multiplication by t_0^{-1} yields (4).

4(b).  Combine det A from (2) with A^{-1} from (4):

 adj A = (det A)\cdot A^{-1}  
     = t_0^{m-r} det(t_0I_r+T V^tU)\cdot t_0^{-1}  
     \times  [I_m - U(t_0T^{-1}+V^tU)^{-1}V^t].  

Using det(t_0I_r+T V^tU)=det T\cdot det(t_0T^{-1}+V^tU) (valid because det T \neq  0)
gives (5).

5.  Additional assumptions: U = V, U^tU = I_r.

A is a sum of symmetric rank-1 matrices, hence symmetric.  Let S := im U
(dim S = r) and S^{\bot } its orthogonal complement.

 *  For x \in  S^{\bot } one has U^tx = 0, so A x = t_0x; every vector of
  S^{\bot } is an eigenvector with eigenvalue t_0.

 *  The columns u^{(k)} (k = 1,\ldots ,r) are an orthonormal basis of S.  For such
  a vector

   A u^{(k)} = t_0u^{(k)} + t_k u^{(k)}(u^{(k)})^tu^{(k)} = (t_0+t_k)u^{(k)},

  because (u^(j^))^tu^{(k)} = 0 for j \neq  k.  Thus u^{(k)} is an eigenvector with
  eigenvalue t_0+t_k.

An orthonormal eigenbasis of \mathbb{R}^m is obtained by adjoining any
orthonormal basis of S^{\bot } to the columns of U, and (6) holds:

 *  \lambda  = t_0 with algebraic = geometric multiplicity m-r;  
 *  For each k,  \lambda  = t_0+t_k  has algebraic = geometric multiplicity 1.

Coincidences among the parameters t_k act as follows:  
Assume t_{k_1}=\cdots =t_{k_s}=\tau  for some \tau  and distinct indices k_1,\ldots ,k_s.  
The vectors u^{(k_1)},\ldots ,u^{(k_s)} are still orthonormal, hence linearly
independent.  Consequently

  \lambda  = t_0+\tau  has algebraic multiplicity s,  
  \lambda  = t_0+\tau  has geometric multiplicity s,

while the multiplicities for the remaining eigenvalues are unchanged.
In the extreme case t_1 = \ldots  = t_r, the spectrum consists of exactly two
eigenvalues,

 t_0 (mult m-r) and t_0+t_1 (mult r),

both with equal algebraic and geometric multiplicities.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Several new parameters (t₀,…,t_r) and up to r rank-one perturbations are introduced, replacing the single parameter and single outer product of the original problem.  
•  The solver must invoke Sylvester’s determinant theorem, factor the characteristic polynomial, trace multiplicities, and handle adjugate matrices—techniques absent from the original statement.  
•  Items (3)–(5) demand spectral analysis, explicit inversion via the Sherman–Morrison–Woodbury formula, and decomposition with respect to an orthogonal direct sum, blending linear algebra, formal algebraic identities, and polynomial factorisation.  
•  The appearance of the full parameter matrix T as well as the assumption VᵗU invertible forces the contestant to juggle non-commutative matrix products, rather than the mere scalar dot-product in the current kernel variant.  
•  Altogether the enhanced variant requires several intertwined ideas—rank considerations, determinant lemmas, eigen-structure, adjugate properties—far beyond the single-line calculation that sufficed previously, and therefore constitutes a substantially harder problem.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let m and r be integers with 1 \leq  r < m.  
For k = 1,\ldots ,r fix (not necessarily distinct) real column-vectors  

  u^{(k)} = (u_1^{(k)},\ldots ,u_m^{(k)})^t,  v^{(k)} = (v_1^{(k)},\ldots ,v_m^{(k)})^t

and assemble the two m \times  r matrices  

  U := [u^{(1)} u^{(2)} \ldots  u^(r^)],  V := [v^{(1)} v^{(2)} \ldots  v^(r^)].

For indeterminates t_0,t_1,\ldots ,t_r put  

  T := diag(t_1,\ldots ,t_r),  A(t_0,\ldots ,t_r) := t_0I_m + U T V^t.     (1)

All algebraic statements below are meant in the polynomial ring  
\mathbb{R}[t_0,\ldots ,t_r, u_i^{(k)}, v_j^{(k)}].

1. (Factorisation of the determinant)  
 Prove that det A is divisible by t_0^{m-r} and that  

  det A(t_0,\ldots ,t_r) = t_0^{\,m-r} det(t_0 I_r + T V^tU).        (2)

2. (Characteristic polynomial)  
 Let \chi _A(\lambda ) := det(\lambda  I_m - A). Show that  

  \chi _A(\lambda ) = (\lambda -t_0)^{\,m-r} \cdot  det[(\lambda -t_0) I_r - T V^tU].      (3)

3. (Spectrum in the general case)  
 Using (3) describe all eigenvalues of A(t_0,\ldots ,t_r) for arbitrary real
choices of the parameters, and give their algebraic multiplicities.

4. From now on impose the additional non-degeneracy conditions  

  det T \neq  0  (i.e. t_k \neq  0 for k = 1,\ldots ,r)  and  det V^tU \neq  0,

and fix a real number t_0 \neq  0 that is not the negative of any eigenvalue of T V^tU  
(equivalently det(t_0 I_r + T V^tU) \neq  0).

 (a)  Prove that A(t_0,\ldots ,t_r) is invertible and establish the
Woodbury-type formula  

  A^{-1} = t_0^{-1}\Bigl[I_m - U\bigl(t_0 T^{-1}+V^tU\bigr)^{-1}V^t\Bigr]. (4)

 (b)  Compute the adjugate of A:

  adj A  
  = t_0^{\,m-r-1} det T \cdot  det\bigl(t_0 T^{-1}+V^tU\bigr)  
    \times  \Bigl[I_m - U\bigl(t_0 T^{-1}+V^tU\bigr)^{-1}V^t\Bigr].     (5)

5. (Symmetric orthonormal specialisation)  
 Assume in addition that U = V and that the r columns of U are
orthonormal, i.e. U^tU = I_r.

 (a)  Prove that A is symmetric and construct an orthonormal basis
of \mathbb{R}^m consisting of eigenvectors of A.

 (b)  Determine the complete spectrum of A and, for every real
specialisation of the parameters, give the algebraic and geometric
multiplicities of the eigenvalues. In particular show that

  spec A = {t_0 (mult m-r)} \cup  {t_0+t_k  (k = 1,\ldots ,r)},     (6)

and discuss in detail how both multiplicities change when some of the
parameters t_k coincide.

Throughout we repeatedly use Sylvester's determinant identity

1.  Put C := t_0^{-1}T.  Then (1) rewrites as

Taking determinants and applying (S) gives

Assume t_{k_1}=\cdots =t_{k_s}=\tau  for some \tau  and distinct indices k_1,\ldots ,k_s.  
The vectors u^{(k_1)},\ldots ,u^{(k_s)} are still orthonormal, hence linearly
independent.  Consequently

  \lambda  = t_0+\tau  has algebraic multiplicity s,  
  \lambda  = t_0+\tau  has geometric multiplicity s,

while the multiplicities for the remaining eigenvalues are unchanged.

both with equal algebraic and geometric multiplicities.
